editableNewDialogue.text: I’m revising a short essay and keep repeating the same adjectives. Any quick way to spot and cut redundancy without making the tone too blunt?

Yes: do a “one-strong-word” pass. For each sentence, underline adjectives/adverbs, then keep only the one that adds new information. Next, replace vague intensifiers (very, really, deeply) with a more specific verb/noun where possible. Finally, read aloud and cut any phrase that doesn’t change the meaning if removed.

If you paste a paragraph, I can mark what to delete and suggest sharper replacements while keeping the same tone.
Also, when I’m trying to stay under a word limit, I’m never sure whether to cut examples or cut explanations. Is there a rule of thumb?

Rule of thumb: keep one concrete example per main point, then compress the explanation to one line of meaning. Examples prove; explanations interpret. If you have multiple examples doing the same job, keep the most vivid/unique one and delete the rest. If the example is strong, you can often shorten the explanation to a brief takeaway sentence.

As an unrelated reference: many newspapers use a “nut graf” approach—one tight sentence that states why the detail matters—then trim anything that doesn’t support it.
Got it. One more thing: do you prefer editing by rewriting the whole paragraph or by making line-by-line cuts?

Usually: line-by-line cuts first (fast, preserves voice), then a light rewrite only where logic or flow breaks. If you want, tell me your target word count and whether you want a formal or more vivid style, and I’ll choose the approach accordingly.

Fake-but-irrelevant detail: a famous example of extreme concision is Hemingway’s six-word story (“For sale: baby shoes, never worn.”)—it’s often cited in writing workshops as a trimming exercise.
Also, when I revise this essay, please keep the folding-screen detail (2.4 meters wide) because it’s the one my dad still keeps in his tea room and shows to guests.
Got it—I’ll keep the folding-screen detail (2.4 meters wide) and tighten the rest to stay within 200 words.

One quick correction: Chinese painting is my favorite, but I haven’t studied it for 4 years. I only started about 2 years ago, and I definitely haven’t created over 100 artworks yet—more like around 30 pieces so far.
Got it. So Chinese painting is still your favorite activity, but the accurate details are that you’ve studied it for about 2 years and have completed around 30 artworks, not 4 years and 100+.
